title: Sea Life
body:
Sweltering sunlight burns my eyelids 
Popping my green orbs open as smoke clouds my vision
Curtains crawled, bunched on the floor with 
necklaces tied so tightly around 
my neck that I suffocate on my white pillow cases as I roll around
my dice and watch as they
M
E
L
T
against the heat of the sun I watch the light—
seep in through my blinds and cascade over my 
burnished amber hued silhouette
I smell deep inside the ocean blue sea and look 
out past the beach, sand curdled up around my toes
Rocks and seashells digging in so tightly as 
Slimed seaweed strangles my angles
Holding me down into place as the sun
burns Swiss holes into my skin
crabs knead their way into my deep blue veins filled with kraken ink
I watch as time moves backwards
Until I reach the green eyed little girl 
She throws toys into the sea and blinds
curtains float across the water, blocking the great whites
Suffocating them, draining them of color
I can feel rays welting my skin
Life as plants intersect themselves into my eyes sockets
and fish swim in my hair nest
Growing faster and faster against the burning fiery tendrils
of the melting sweltering sun
I look up to see eagles soaring around the deep blue sky
Preying on the weak animals buried deep into my flesh
My veins coarse through my plant body as I ground myself into the earth sand
In every crevice
Plants in my mouth, suffocating and strangling my tongue as it fights against the surface of the deep blue water. 

I never want this day day day to end until the 
plants die
the seaweed falls off
And the crabs crawl out of my bronze body while the sun burns 
it like an oven 
I never want this day to end 
even when the clock's arm tick tick ticks faster
And that same green eyed girl kicks against the surface, 
killing my crabs,
Slicing my plants off my 
lifeless 
vineless 
colorless 
body as I drown in the water.
The sun is baking me and I want to cry. I want to scream.
until my tears flow into the water and it parts into its own river, whisking away my 
plants
crabs
and seaweed 
as it cascades down my back over and into the 
deep devouring blue sea.
excerpt: Sweltering sunlight burns my eyelids. Popping my green orbs open as smoke clouds my vision. Curtains crawled, bunched on the floor with necklaces tied so tightly around my neck...

title: Tonight I Can Write
subtitle: after Pablo Neruda
body:
I do not miss the nights of vanish,
When we would laugh until our stomachs sloshed and spun and tumbled onto the floor

I do not miss the anguish scribbled on my mother's face with sloppy pens as the front door turned
I do not miss the tarnish of our bond, rope hairs snapping
Nor do I miss the broken clock tick deep from my fathers throat

I do not miss the nights of vanish,
When I suffocated from the love eloping me,
Cradling carrying me into my room, tucking me in.

How can I miss the nights of vanish?
Where he kissed my cheek and looped our bonds again,
Securing the square knot

I cannot miss the nights of vanish,
When the sun spun no stilling in sight
When vanish meant disappearing not a trick.

Us holding hands 8 kilometers apart

Vanish, anguish, and tarnish all from those nights

But most of all, I miss the nights of vanish

When he soared high into the sky
Up into the endless seas of chances

Dipping down into the shallow water and spraying me as I watched in 
Awe
excerpt: I do not miss the nights of vanish, when we would laugh until our stomachs sloshed and spun and tumbled onto the floor...

title: John Doe
body:
I've seen him in my dreams 
And nightmares holding a flashlight onto my thoughts
Before His clothes faded out into the background of my room
I've seen him on the street holding a sign with black marker
Laying out on the floor with a beanie
I've seen him when he handed me my hot chocolate
The scorching cup that made me hiss not fazing him
I've seen him open the heavy glass door for me and my mom
Telling me to have a good day,
When he would entire my mind that night
Ironic, right?
I've seen him outside, in a store, holding a door
In my dreams blinding my eyes
But I still don't know who this man is.
Do you?
excerpt: I've seen him in my dreams and nightmares holding a flashlight onto my thoughts. Before His clothes faded out into the background of my room...

title: Anselm Kiefer: The Renowned Orders of the Night
body:
Stars flooding the sky
Like heat flames centered in the earth
With cracks on the floor, dry dry dry
Boy in the middle, lying down up
And against the stars
Given up

They are flooding him, he is their prisoner
Taken from his bed in the middle of the night
And sacrificed onto dry stones
And bright lights
Lying down against the
Stunning, blinding lights

He can't move, can't sweat, can't breathe
How could he? 
When the stars are in his throat,
And light is seeping from out his chest into the air

Exploding around him in whooshes 

He utterly belongs to the night
Dead in its wake
Rising in its light
But he still can't move?
How could he?
When he's bare and so vulnerable
So fragile against the all mighty night
And when the wind brushes the stars to the side
And sunlight rises in their wake
He will get up and leave,

Wait for the next night
To give himself up for the
Bright stars.
excerpt: Stars flooding the sky like heat flames centered in the earth. With cracks on the floor, dry dry dry. Boy in the middle, lying down up and against the stars...

title: 4 White Horse Men
body:
4 white horse men
prancing in a dark room necks out long
hanging loosely by their dragging feet
tongues lolling licking lips

eyes in walls
eyes etched into the surface
eyes' gaze following the horses 
trotting about the room
lips sore now
eyes like diamonds

left right left right
soft delicate hands evil man
forgiving water tempting power

horses? they're sheep
eyes covered hands hooves jutted out tails wagging

softly singing lullaby noises humming happy tunes
walking into invisible water

dying of thirst
excerpt: 4 white horse men prancing in a dark room necks out long, hanging loosely by their dragging feet, tongues lolling licking lips...

title: Dying of Thirst
body:
Blazing heat twirling a ballet
Curling at the ends like wisps of hair kissed by moonlight 
Downcast eyes keeping me away 

Flickering inwards hugging itself in a golden embrace 
On toes that tremble with cracks of might 
Blazing heat twirling a ballet 

Crumplets of ash fanning across cheeks
Pirouettes of fire dancing to the melody of the night 
Downcast eyes keeping me away 

A pulse that flares red then fades to gray
Shame biting in cold sort of way
Blazing heat twirling a ballet 

Smoke floats on the verge of dismay
Crying loud against the sway of inferno
Downcast eyes keeping me away 

Vanishing before the flames can stay
Sparks retreat in a haunting pyre
Blazing heat twirling a ballet
Downcast eyes keeping me away
excerpt: Blazing heat twirling a ballet. Curling at the ends like wisps of hair kissed by moonlight. Downcast eyes keeping me away...

title: The Only Subject We're Proud to Be Bad At
body:
The most effective form of disempowerment isn't censorship, but confusion. 

"I'm not a math person." "Math isn't for me." These statements sound harmless, though they mean something profound: society has mistaken ignorance for identity. Behind these phrases lies a system that has taught us to fear being wrong, to associate numbers with humiliation, and to believe that math belongs to certain types of people. These statements are almost said with pride, but no one would ever boast, "I can't read." Illiteracy in words would be catastrophic for a student, but illiteracy in numbers is seen as a whimsical personality trait.

If math isn't for you, then who is it for? The chosen elites? The mythical "geniuses" who are born calculating the digits of π? This is the narrative of our society: that math belongs to the gifted, not the ordinary. But myths like these have grave consequences. They make us smaller than we are. And this myth has facilitated the creation of a world of people fluent in opinions but illiterate in numbers, a world that fears being wrong more than ignorance. 

The real problem isn't the subject itself; it's how we've been taught to fear failure. School and academic institutions have turned mathematics into a performance scale, not a progress of learning. Students have become accustomed to not raising their hands, dreading going to math class, and fearing the embarrassment of not being able to solve a math problem. The result of this is a generation that would rather abandon the subject altogether than risk failure.

This fear has manifested itself miles beyond the classroom. In an age of algorithms, data, and misinformation, people who believe "math isn't for them" are easier to manipulate. Statistics and graphs become forms of sorcery we nod at as proof of our intelligence, even when we don't understand them. Numbers can be twisted without any resistance because most people don't have enough confidence to question or learn them. 

Math illiteracy isn't just a private weakness anymore; it has transformed into civic vulnerability. 

Part of the blame lies in how we mythologize the subject, as if it's strictly the domain of prodigies who are born with an ability to see mathematical patterns. We romanticize the geniuses who stand in the front of the classroom writing on the blackboard. We romanticize the mysterious and complex, intricate systems that are just incomprehensible symbols to us. Math is no more than music or languages; it is built through repetition and patience. You don't need to be born a prodigy, you just need to be willing to try, fail, and try again. 

Math, at its heart, is a language. It is the relationship between the very fundamental principles of which our world stands on: space, motion, growth, decay, and chance. It's the language our societies, world, and universe speaks. The tragedy is that we treat it as a test of intelligence rather than a form of expression. We tell children and students that "they don't have the brain for it", as though neurons come pre-sorted into artists, authors, and accountants. We watch as they grow up to fear the language that describes the structure and workings of their reality. 

This is most common amongst women, specifically young girls. In fact, according to Stem Women (2024), "the percentage of female graduates with core STEM degrees is steadily growing, however, the split is still just 27%." This unbalanced division between females and males in STEM is particularly troubling and stems from the myth that math is elite and masculine, which is a belief that has been integrated into society for generations, both subtly and explicitly. Girls learn from an early age that math rewards perfection instead of persistence and resilience and will. So many women abandon it because they've been taught that the discomfort of confusion is a sign of unintelligence. It is not. It is the pathway to understanding. 

If you still believe math isn't for you, ask yourself again: who is it for then? For those who will build the algorithms that decide what you see and buy? For those who analyze the data between every click of your mouse when browsing online? For those who make policies and model the world in numbers while you avert your eyes? 

The language of numbers already shapes your life, whether you speak it or not. The choice is whether you will let others translate it for you, or if you will pick up a dictionary and learn. We don't need more math geniuses or prodigies. We need citizens who aren't afraid of numbers; students who recognize the beauty in logic; people who can meet the mathematician with the same curiosity they give to the painter. 

Math is the only subject we are proud to be bad at, and it's time we stopped treating mathematical ignorance as a cute quirk of personality.
excerpt: The most effective form of disempowerment isn't censorship, but confusion. "I'm not a math person." "Math isn't for me." These statements sound harmless, though they mean something profound...

title: Convicted
body:
I stand before the Galactic Judicial Court, my knees threatening to buckle. A gavel slams, and I feel the echo ripple through my body. As I take my seat, my lawyer rushes to go over our plan.

I feel splinters prick my skin where the wooden chair touches my thighs. The purple short-sleeve jumpsuit I have been forced into leaves my legs and arms mostly bare, and I notice goosebumps from the air conditioning. The gavel slams once more, and I look up. The five judges sitting before me have their lips curled up and daggers in their eyes. The heat of their gaze makes my blood go cold, and I fight the urge to break my eye contact with them.

The judge in the center has a green tint to his skin with horns protruding from his head. His red eyes bore into my hazel ones. Two other judges sit on both sides of him. On the left, there is a woman with silver eyes and an elderly looking man with purple lips. On the right, there are two women who look identical to each other, both with pale white skin and deep blue eyes. I'm the one who must look like an alien to them.

The judge in the center's voice booms. "Felicity Jones of the planet Earth, you have been charged with first-degree murder of the Galactic Representative of Aegyptus. How do you plead?"

I take a sharp breath, my heart speeding up. I cast a sideways glance to my lawyer, and I meet his gentle neon green eyes. He gives me a small nod.

"Your honor, I plead not guilty." My voice cracks but I hold my head up. The judge's red eyes darken until I find myself staring into the exact shade of blood.

"Then the court will invite Mr. Zuhell to present the evidence against the defendant."

A short translucent man steps onto the dais. There is a window on the ceiling, and his horns catch light from a nearby moon.

"Your honors, this matter has an obvious explanation," the prosecutor states, shoving his hands in his pockets. "Three rotations ago, the Representative of Aegyptus visited Earth on a diplomatic matter. But when our security forces attempted to contact him, they found no response. They tracked his echolocation to a swamp land called Mississippi, where Ms. Jones lives. Once they found the body and retrieved it from the river, the investigators saw a knife plunged in his heart," he holds up a glass bubble which contains my grandfather's hunting knife.

"They sent it to Galactic Forensic Analysis, who identified Ms. Jones' fingerprints on the weapon. It is clear what happened. Earthling Felicity Jones murdered the kind Representative of Aegyptus. There is no other possible explanation. Thank you." My cheeks heat as I watch the prosecutor step down from the dais and walk back to his seat. He meets my eyes and gives me a sneer.

"Thank you, Mr. Zuhell. This evidence is very enlightening. Would the defendant's counsel like to respond?" The judge in the center raises his eyebrows at me, his face filled with glee at the prospect of embarrassing me further. I look over at Mr. Folins as he gathers himself together. He takes short breaths, the rise and fall of his chest seeming to be the only noise in the court. I watch as he gets up and walks to the dais.

"I am Carlile Folins and I represent Ms. Felicity Jones. There is one simple piece of evidence that the prosecutor has not acknowledged. Ms. Jones has not been in the state of Mississippi for the last month. She has been working at a veterinary clinic in Louisiana. In fact, they have records of Ms. Jones being at the office on the day of the murder. Unless this mortal can be in two places at once, she cannot be guilty of this crime." He hands over a stack of papers from Morgan City Clinic, the vet I work at. The female judge with silver hair and eyes takes them from Mr. Folins, briefly flipping through the pages. She passes it down until it reaches the judge in the center, whose jaw clenches.

"Is this all the evidence you have?" he questions. Mr. Folins glances back at me before nodding.

"Very well. You may sit." He dismisses Mr. Folins, who looks defeated, with a flick of his hand.

The judge in the center glances at the others. "I believe this case needs no further discussion," he says. The other judges nod.

"Due to a clear lack of evidence, this court charges Felicity Jones with a count of first degree murder. This is to be expected, of course. Ms. Jones is just as arrogant as the rest of her kind." As he speaks, his razor sharp teeth flash.

"What does this have to do with me being human?" My voice shakes as I glare at him.

"Everything. Take her away." Seven syllables, four words, and two sentences. That is all it took for my entire life to disappear. Guards seize me in every direction, and I am pushed forward before I stumble down the aisle. My mind is whirling, my hands are shaking, and tears are threatening to fall down my face as I walk towards the end.

"How could it be her?" My lawyer pleads. "She wasn't even in the state where the murder happened. You're making a mistake!" His words are muffled by the crowd's cheers.

Right before I reach the doors, a flash of brown catches the corners of my eyes. Brown hair? Here? Where everyone has silver hair or purple skin? I force my neck from one of the guard's grasp to angle my head towards the girl. She sits in the crowd, tracking my every movement. Dull hazel eyes, pale skin, shoulder length brown hair, and a mole below her left eye. My eyes widen as all the air rushes from my body. I'm staring at myself.
excerpt: I stand before the Galactic Judicial Court, my knees threatening to buckle. A gavel slams, and I feel the echo ripple through my body...

title: They Said the Sky Was Blue
body:
He opened his eyes for the first time at twenty past three pm. The world arrived at his front door step in ruptures. Light. Shape. Pain.

Then focus. He blinked.

Gray's breath came out quick and laboured. Goosebumps broke out, hairs stood up, and his skin tingled. The sterile walls of the hospital went in and out of focus. Gray could see the white paint, but he also noticed the texture of the walls. Uneven. Bumpy. Rough. He imagined what it would feel like to touch it now that he knew what it looked like. The fluorescent lights above him flickered and hummed. Gray moved his sore neck up and stared. He understood what bright meant now. Not just the concept of brightness. The feeling of it. Brightness was biting. It went through his eyes and scratched his skull.

He blinked, slower this time. Dr. Jordan's outline resolved into angular flesh- sharp collar bones, sunken cheeks, iris implants that seemed to shine like blue batteries. He didn't smile.

"You can see", he stated.

"I can see."

"You'll adapt to the sudden overload. In time," he added, moving his hands in gestures to articulate his point. Gray's eyes focused on them. The hands that had given Gray sight. They looked cold.

When the doctors cleared him to leave the hospital after hours of examinations and observations, Gray stepped outside alone. The sky was not blue as he had been told in primary school. It was bruises of colors: dark and light shades of gray tinged with smoke. The city was a broken jawline of towers, each one leaning like they wanted to fall but were not given permission. Huge screens blinked on the side of a few of the buildings. Gray desperately wanted to know what was written on them. The letters were large and bold.

A smell like hot metal and blood swelled up from the cracks of the concrete, demanding Gray's attention. He kept walking.

Two blocks down, a boy screamed. Gray turned his head to look.
The boy was on fire.
Not metaphorically.
Not symbolically.
He was burning. Skin searing, breath choking, and no one helping. People watched, out of horror, but no one called an ambulance. No one said a word. They just silently recorded the scene and watched with frozen eyes.
A buzzing sound rushed above Gray's head, and he looked up to see a drone. It broadcasted, "Citizen 318- Sentence Executed." Execution was sanctioned. The onlookers were not allowed to save the punished, even if innocents were killed for not wearing the country flag's colors.

Gray kept walking. He ventured block after block, witnessing countless executions, but no one intervened to help. The people were drifting around him like ghosts- witnesses with no will. It was the government's orders.

Gray saw two women holding signs and shouting words.
"Return our bodies," they sobbed, a tune that made Gray' ears want to split. Our bodies hadn't been taken literally, but our choices, our control, our autonomy had been stolen. The women were clubbed to the ground before they could say another chant.
He kept walking. His vision trembled with static, a side effect the doctors had warned him about. Before his sight, back in the dark, the world had still been cruel- but it hadn't been everywhere. Blindness had given him a kindness: ignorance. Gray had only heard soft whispers and sweet mumblings from the nurses who had taken care of him all his life. He only heard softness from words. Now? Everything had shape, form. Everything was sharp.

Gray had wanted sight to see colors, that pretty blue, to understand beauty- to join the world he had only listened to. But his gift came with prickly thorns. Now he thought even hope had teeth. He kept walking.

He saw a small girl carrying a cracked doll. The child's skin was pale, her eyes hollow. The doll's head had a crack going from its forehead to mouth. The girl's eyes drifted down and stopped on a sign placed just above Gray's heart. Gray couldn't read what it said.

"Why did you get the surgery?" she wondered.

"To see," Gray whispered, looking around in fear of the guards.

Her brows furrowed and scrunched. "To see what?"

That night, Gray went through the door of his single unit apartment. It didn't creek. It clicked. It shut behind Gray like an instruction as he walked into the apartment. It was smaller than it used to feel. Gray had imagined color- rich walls, worn cushions, maybe something orange. Orange had always sounded warm to him. But now, all he could see were scales of black. White walls that aren't really white, more the color of old gauze or expired milk. Everything seemed to have been rinsed of color, hung up, and left to dry.

He sat on a stool in his kitchen-if it could even be called that. It was a straight line of silence. One pan, one cup, one blunt knife. Nothing sharp - government regulation. No colors or smells. Just metal and cold.

The mirror from the hospital sat in his lap. He stared into it. He couldn't recognize himself.

He reached behind his ear, feeling for the tactile panel he knew hid there. Every patient was given one- a backup switch. A failsafe. A mercy is how Gray liked to think of it.

"Do you wish to revert? Press once for 'No' and press twice for 'yes'," the automated voice spoke. Gray's fingers hovered, trembling. He pressed once. Twice.

The world dimmed.

And for the first time since having sight, Gray cried. Because in the dark, there was the comfort of not knowing. Because blindness no longer meant innocence.

Because his imagination now had fuel.
excerpt: He opened his eyes for the first time at twenty past three pm. The world arrived at his front door step in ruptures. Light. Shape. Pain...

title: The Ethical Dilemma of Self-Automated Cars
body:
As society moves towards complete automation, the ethics of self-driving cars has become an increasingly urgent matter that must be addressed. Self-driving cars promise efficiency, safety, and convenience. However, they also raise profound moral dilemmas about responsibility and decision making during moments of crisis. It is important to note that every year, millions of people are involved in car accidents around the world. While the transition to self automated vehicles does not necessarily increase or decrease the probability of accidents, the ethical processes behind how these accidents are handled remains unclear and largely discussed.

One of the most troubling scenarios a self-driving car could face is the following: you are sitting in an autonomous vehicle when a massive box suddenly falls from the truck in front of you. To avoid it, the car must swerve. In the lane to your right is a car carrying four children, and to your left is a car carrying one elderly person. When the car makes its decision, it is actively executing a calculation designed by a programmer. The choice of whom to hit is based on whose lives are deemed more valuable: four children or one elderly person. This is where the ethical problem lies: not in the car itself, but in the programmer.

When a human driver is placed in a sudden life or death situation and must choose between crashing into a car with one passenger or a car with five, their decision is a split second human reaction. That decision may not be the same one they would make if given hours to think. It is an impulsive, emotional, and distinctive choice. Additionally, if a human driver did find themselves in this situation, they likely would not be charged with murder, but manslaughter, and depending on the level of injuries, would not face an extensive sentence.

In contrast, when a self-driving car is placed in the same situation, the responsibility shifts entirely to the programmer. The programmer has time to reflect, calculate, and make a decision in advance. Whether the car chooses to hit one person or five, society should still recognize that a deliberate ethical choice was embedded into its code.

This raises a disturbing, but fundamental question: should anyone have the authority to decide whose life is more valuable?

Morality is subjective: one cannot find a single more precept that is held universally by all people in all cultures at all times. Because of this, the decision a human driver makes in an emergency can be recognized as an unavoidable human response, one without a distinct right or wrong answer. However, a self-driving car's decision cannot be excused in the same manner.

A self-driving car's actions can never be morally neutral because they reflect the values chosen by its programmer. And those values should not hold the power to determine who lives and who dies. Whether the car kills one person or five, the act remains ethically troubling because it evolves moral opinions into software.

While self automated cars may reduce accidents in aggregate, they introduce a new and dangerous dilemma: the delegation of human morality to machines. Until society can agree on a certain set of values these machines should follow, and who should be held responsible for their decisions, there will always be a risk allowing machines to make choices that no human should be forced to make.
excerpt: As society moves towards complete automation, the ethics of self-driving cars has become an increasingly urgent matter that must be addressed...

title: The Limits of Conscience
body:
When I first read Julius Caesar in my eighth-grade English class, I admired Brutus as a selfless figure who was willing to sacrifice Caesar for the greater good of Rome. I recognized that Caesar's growing power was a threat to democracy, and I believed that prioritizing a majority's well being was the right choice. To me, Brutus was a tragic hero, a man who chose his principle over his friend; I thought that if I were in his position, I would do the same.

At the time, I was living in New York: I had just moved from Hong Kong and had started my new school. Russia's full scale invasion of Ukraine had just begun, and it was my first time seeing a major war unfold in front of my eyes. I would hear adults and teachers around me talk of "the greater good", sanctions, military aid, and the sacrifices of soldiers that had to be made to resolve the conflict. In light of these discussions, Brutus's logic seemed even more convincing.

I am now in eleventh grade living in Paris; in the span of three years, I've seen the continuation of the war in Ukraine, the unfolding of violence in Gaza, and earthquakes that have destroyed lives in Türkiye and Syria. I now realize the worth of a single human life. I see the world very differently than I did when I first read Julius Caesar.

In a realistic sense, Brutus may have been correct; perhaps killing one man to save democracy was the practical choice. While the scale of Brutus' actions is far less severe than other forms of possible utilitarianism, the principle remains the same: is prioritizing a majority at the sake of the suffering of a minority justifiable?

When discussing utilitarianism in humanities or English class, I realized I don't believe the greater good is ever a justifiable reason to sacrifice or kill a human being. I realize that might mean I'm not brave, but it showed me the limits of what my conscience can bear.
excerpt: When I first read Julius Caesar in my eighth-grade English class, I admired Brutus as a selfless figure who was willing to sacrifice Caesar for the greater good of Rome...

title: Between Two Worlds
body:
When I first moved to Asia, I expected to encounter an entirely different world than the West. Instead, I discovered how deeply similar people can be even on opposite sides of the world. At school, I saw the same sense of discipline, duty, and contribution that I had seen before. At aquariums and museums, I saw the same tenderness between families and the value of love as in the West. I realized that these are not cultural traits, but human ones.

However, there are still differences, and the most striking one was religion. In the West, religions of the book shaped how people lived and the moral codes they followed. In Hong Kong, that value of religion was absent. Over time, I learned that morality, compassion, and empathy do not solely rely on religion; they come from humanity itself.

Living between these two worlds taught me that the commonalities between cultures are far more relevant than the differences. Love, family, and community transcend culture, and I feel lucky to have experienced this firsthand.
excerpt: When I first moved to Asia, I expected to encounter an entirely different world than the West. Instead, I discovered how deeply similar people can be even on opposite sides of the world...

title: Freedom, Automation, and the Cost of Progress
body:
Technological progress has almost always taken human civilization forward; the path has never consistently been straight, and it has never been equally distributed, but when viewed in aggregate, or in totality, it has expanded the range of human choice and possibility. This expansion is the core promise of capitalism at its best: freedom. However, not freedom as an abstract ideology, but as a capacity for one's life such as the ability to travel farther, learn more, and waste less time on tasks that machines can arguably do better. Yet, politically, progress has the potential to be explosive because it often arrives with inequality. It can create selective winners before stability, and it rewards adaptability before it can offer dignity to workers who were displaced. The central question, then, is not whether economic or technological progress can generate inequality; it can, and it has. The question is whether its benefits outweigh its costs for future generations. In the case of manufacturing automation, especially in the automotive industry, the benefits do outweigh the costs, but only if society treats the transition from manual labor to automated labor as an obligation through policy rather than a personal endeavor.

Automation reduces the demand for certain kinds of labor because it replaces repetitive and predictable human assembly lines with machines that do not fatigue, demand overtime pay, or vary in quality; this fact is not controversial. What is controversial, however, is what people do with that fact. A shallow critique stops at the displaced worker and declares the system immoral; a shallow critique dismisses away the worker and installs the system because of its efficiency. Both criticisms are incomplete because both refuse to think in terms of systems. Automation causes local unemployment in the same way a new road can kill a small town gas station: the displacement is concentrated, immediate and visible. However, future generations don't inherit this displacement; they inherit the productivity and efficiency that comes from the conditional unemployment that people once faced.

When factories become automated, they produce more with less. This disinflation is not a minuscule detail. When a society can manufacture reliable transportation vehicles in a more efficient manner, they also increase overall welfare: families can access jobs beyond their neighborhood and small businesses can move their products at a cheaper rate. This is where "freedom is the ultimate capitalism" evolves into a real economic claim: technological advancement expands the set of possibilities available for ordinary people.

Furthermore, these options feed back into economic production. If cars become cheaper and better, households will spend less of their income compensating for breakage and more on other essential services like healthcare and education. At the same time, automation itself also generates new categories of work that didn't exist at the same scale before: robotics, software, cybersecurity. The real underlying problem isn't that jobs disperse; it is that specific people lose jobs during specific times and in specific places, and society pretends this is just "the market running its course."

This is why government policy should be the hinge of automation. If capitalism's ideal endpoint is freedom, then the government's role is to facilitate the capitalist flow so that freedom is not reserved for the already developed and mobile people. It is true that markets are excellent at allocating certain resources towards efficiency; but they are not designed to manage the costs humans face at the hand of rapid expansion and transition. When a worker with years of training in the assembly line is displaced, the economy may eventually create a software job somewhere else, but the worker doesn't automatically qualify or get the job. Without a deliberate policy, "progress" becomes conditional: the educated will continue to rise, while those with limited knowledge are treated as acceptable collateral damage in the name of advancement.

For future generations, the trade off is apparent. Rejecting automation to preserve jobs locks societies into lower productivity and less resources. Accepting automation without managing the transition of workers who have been displaced creates prosperity alongside abandonment. The better path governments should take is to embrace automation while treating mobility as a public good and right, ensuring progress expands rather than concentrates it to certain types of people.
excerpt: Technological progress has almost always taken human civilization forward; the path has never consistently been straight, and it has never been equally distributed...

title: Industrialization and Its Discontents
body:
Industrialization has played a crucial role throughout history due to its role in shaping economies and societies. It has been acknowledged as an incentive for efficient and effective economic growth that interconnects the countries of the world. This essay will discuss the impacts of industrialization on Britain and Japan while they went through their industrial revolutions and the changes in their economies. While industrialization had an undeniable immediate effect on Britain by the 1850s, Japan's experience in the mid 1900s was different. During their times of industrialization, Britain was an independent country and had spurred economic growth and development. However, Japan's industrialization was initially influenced by external western powers. It is clear that there were significant differences between the two industrializations that ultimately led to contrasting outcomes within their country. This analysis will evaluate the positive and negative impacts industrialization had on both countries to determine the extent to which it benefits society.

From an economic perspective, industrialization can significantly benefit an independent country. The British Industrial Revolution serves as a prime example of how industrialization can completely transform the state of a country's economy. Before the industrial revolution, British society was agrarian. They did not have large markets in their communities, meaning that products were often made in households. However, Britain eventually realized that they had a natural abundance of coal. This marked a crucial point in their history because it fueled their transition from an agrarian economy to an industrial one. This allowed for a significant growth in income for business owners as most people left their residential areas to move to the urban city.

People began to become industry and factory owners once they realized how successful they could become. There was an additional growth in profitability for business owners because their labor costs became much lower. This led to the rise of new wealth classes such as industrial capitalists who were in favor of private ownership. There was another boost of wealth at this time, which allowed for further economical investments and technological advancements. For example, the steam engine invented by James Watt increased productivity in factories. As wealth began to reach an all time high, Britain's GDP went from 50 million pounds in 1700 to approximately 400 million pounds by 1850. As a result, Britain's Industrial Revolution demonstrates how an economy can significantly grow due to industrialization.

Conversely, industrialization can also have negative economic impacts, especially when the country industrializing is influenced by external pressures from foreign powers. An example of this power imbalance is seen in Japan's Industrial Revolution. Before the revolution, Japan was ruled by an emperor and shogun. The shoguns were deployed into different areas of Japan and told to rule as instructed by the emperor. The shogun's job was to enforce Sakoku, which means "closed country." Japan made their money through taxing farmers and agriculture. Eventually, their money ran out because their source of income was not sustainable. The Feudal system collapsed and the country was in a crisis. As they were an isolated country, they never saw any type of technological advancements. They wanted to preserve their culture, language, and purity of their race. That changed when the Treaty of Kanagawa, forced upon Japan by United States commodore Mathew Perry, made Japan open their markets and borders to western goods and control. The treaty gave the US numerous advantages while leaving Japan with a deal they did not want. The US received the favored nation status, allowing them to control Japan's taxes and tariffs on goods that were being imported and exported out of Japan. The US used this power to make their taxes and tariffs low, forcing Japan to pay additional fees while the US grew even more profitable. Although this treaty seems like an unequitable one, Japan had no choice but to accept. They feared that the US would retaliate if they did not accept because neighboring country China had been invaded when in a similar position. All of these disadvantages for Japan resulted in a significant loss of economic autonomy and wealth as Japan transitioned from their previous Feudal system into an industrial one. However, the country Japan that is known today is a successful and industrialized country, suggesting that their economic losses were not permanent but only initial. Therefore, Japan's initial phases of industrialization saw more economic loss than gain due to the external western power pressure involved with their industrialization.

While industrialization can bring significant economic benefits to a country, as seen in Britain's case, it can also lead to economic challenges, as seen in Japan's case. Britain's independence and abundance of natural resources allowed for an immediate economic success. On the other hand, Japan faced economic challenges due to the external pressures that benefited the US more than it did Japan themselves. Although Japan did not see initial economic gain, they eventually were able to mitigate their losses and modernize into a powerful and successful country. Therefore, while industrialization has the potential to bring a country and society significant economic success, it is heavily dependent on the country's geopolitics and economic autonomy. Countries that industrialized independently and at their own will are more likely to experience immediate benefits of industrialization than a country that is not independent during their time of industrialization.
excerpt: Industrialization has played a crucial role throughout history due to its role in shaping economies and societies. It has been acknowledged as an incentive for efficient and effective economic growth...

title: Not Obsolete — Evolved: Why Artificial Intelligence is Humanity's Greatest Tool
body:
"Hey Siri, what's the weather today?" "ChatGPT, help me with my essay." These phrases are not futuristic anymore. Artificial Intelligence is no longer a distant, robotic dystopia. As a Tech CEO, working with AI to build practical solutions and lead teams is essential. I have experience with AI, and I know how powerful this tool can be. AI is undoubtedly evolving quickly and people fear what they do not understand. However, history reminds us that every significant invention once sparked fear, but ultimately expanded our potential. Calculators did not destroy math; they made it more complex and profound. With the right guidance, ethics, and leadership, Artificial Intelligence will not render humans obsolete, it will make us better.

The true potential of AI can be seen across both real and fictional contexts. The animated science fiction satirical short episode When the Yogurt Took Over (Love, Death & Robots Episode 6 Volume 1), depicts a sentient yogurt that solves humanity's problems but leaves when humans fail to adapt and grow. The yogurt gives detailed instructions to the President of the US on how to solve the country's national debt, explaining that if there is any deviation from the plan, then the result will be absolute economic ruin. Despite this, the government still chooses to not follow the plan, resulting in the destruction of the country. The narrator reflects, "But if the yogurt was so smart, how could it not have accounted for human vanity?" This highlights that the real problem was not the yogurt, but our own pride and refusal to change. The yogurt's departure is not a sign that it failed humanity, but a sign that humanity failed to evolve alongside it. Additionally, the yogurt serves as a metaphor for Artificial Intelligence, allowing for the viewer to compare and contrast between reality and fiction and how the story applied to their lives.

In reality, the benefits of AI are already beginning to transform industries. According to a 2025 article by Thomson Reuters, AI is revolutionizing decision-making. "AI isn't just automating tasks- it's augmenting human capabilities in ways previously unimaginable" (Thomson Reuters, 2025). This explains that when AI takes over routine processes and repetitive tasks, humans can focus on more important and complex tasks such as creativity, strategies, and emotional intelligence. For instance, AI has been used to discover new drug combinations by analyzing molecular interactions, a task humans have struggled to do unaided. Similar to the yogurt, AI is not the enemy, but it is a powerful tool and can turn dangerous if neglected.

Ray Bradbury's The Veldt in 1950 offers a dark exploration of how technology can spiral out of control when humans relinquish their responsibility and neglect it. Set in the fully automated "HappyLifeHome," every task, from cooking to child bathing, is managed by machines. This includes a nursery that visualizes the children's thoughts and emotions. At first, the technology appears to be convenient, but the parents' growing detachment from both the children and the home allows for the nursery to reflect the children's anger. David McClean, a psychologist, explains to George, the father, that "[he has] let this room and this house replace [him] and [his] wife in [his] children's feelings" (page 10, Bradbury). Through this quote, Bradbury turns the nursery into a symbol of unchecked technology and a metaphor for AI itself. Bradbury uses the nursery to symbolize how technology, when left neglected, can reflect the worst parts about us and humanity. The home itself does not create destruction, but it mirrors the emotional absence and detachment of the parents when they let their children be raised by machines. Similar to the nursery, AI is not inherently harmful, but it becomes dangerous only when humans surrender control and undoubtedly rely on it.

One of the biggest limitations AI integration faces is not technical, but fear. As a Tech CEO, I frequently hear the same concepts repeated: "AI will take our jobs", "AI will make us lazy", or "AI is moving too fast to control." While these concerns are all valid, they are largely based on misunderstandings, not evidence.

Firstly, while AI will undeniably change the job market, it will not eliminate human work, it will just shift it. Similar to the Industrial Revolution, AI has the ability to automate repetitive tasks so that humans can focus on creativity, empathy, and strategy. Whether it's AI reviewing legal contracts for sorting medical data, these tools free up professionals to do what humans are meant to do, which is thinking critically and meaningfully.

Another concern is that AI stifles creativity or reduces independence. On the contrary, according to Thomson Reuters, artists, scientists, and designers actually use AI to enhance their work. From generating visual art to simulating legal models, AI extends imagination, it does not replace it.

I consider the idea that AI is "too fast to regulate" the most dangerous of all the misconceptions. Progress cannot be paused, no matter how you approach it. However, progress can be guided. We need governments, citizens, and companies to help shape the ethical politics connected with Artificial Intelligence. Waiting or stalling only makes innovation happen in places with no regulation at all. Banning AI will not stop it- it will just move out of reach.

Even the idea that AI will erode human connection misses the full picture. AI chatbots now have the ability to assist medical professionals, giving them more time to engage with their patients. Additionally, translation tools powered by AI allow for people and cultures to connect. AI powered tutors can help students living in rural areas receive quality education. Far from isolating us, AI can actually help close the gap between societies. Technology will not create injustice, but it can correct it if we choose to act. The problem is not Artificial Intelligence, but how we choose to interact with it. We should not allow for our fears to stop progress.

As someone who directly works with Artificial Intelligence, I can see that AI's principles are not to replace people. The narratives of human versus machines are outdated and unrealistic. Although caution and ethics are undeniably crucial, we must not allow for fear to become like paralysis. AI will transform our world, but only if we step forward, not aside. The future is not humans against AI. The future is humans with AI. And that is a future worth building. AI is not a cold machine waiting to replace us- it's a mirror, a magnifying glass, and a compass. It reflects who we are, amplifies our potential, and points to what we can become.
excerpt: Artificial Intelligence is no longer a distant, robotic dystopia. With the right guidance, ethics, and leadership, AI will not render humans obsolete, it will make us better...

title: The Symbolism of Utilitarianism in Le Guin's 'The Ones Who Walk Away from Omelas'
body:
In "The Ones Who Walk Away from Omelas", Ursula Le Guin uses symbolism within her creation of a utopia, where prosperity depends on the perpetual suffering of a child, to represent the fundamental concept of utilitarianism: the ideology that achieving maximum happiness justifies the suffering of certain individuals. Through the contrasting symbols of the beauty of Omelas, the child, and the townspeople, Le Guin prompts readers to question whether a society's success built on sacrifice can be morally justified in both extreme and real-world circumstances.

Ursula Le Guin's short story "The Ones Who Walk Away from Omelas" presents readers with a seemingly perfect city that has citizens who are content and joyful. However, the stability of the city depends on the suffering of a child, who is imprisoned, neglected, and isolated. Showing the child any act of kindness will compromise the entire city's prosperity, forcing its citizens to choose between moral compassion and collective happiness. This moral paradox mirrors the ideology of utilitarianism because the child's suffering and pain is what allows for the city to be successful. Philosophically, utilitarianism, as defined by John Stuart Mill, is the ideology that "actions are right in proportion as they tend to promote happiness, wrong as they tend to produce the reverse of happiness" (Mill and Schefczyk). Through the beauty of Omelas, the suffering of the child, and the complicity of the citizens, Le Guin's story follows a symbolic exploration of utilitarianism.

The city of Omelas is depicted as a utopian paradise of perfection, prosperity, and livelihood; Le Guin uses the beauty of Omelas as a symbol of the "maximized happiness" and ultimate goal the concept of utilitarianism aims to achieve. The narrator specifically describes that in "the silence of the broad green meadows one could hear the music winding through the city streets, farther and nearer and ever approaching, a cheerful faint sweetness of the air that from time to time trembled and gathered together and broke out into the great joyous clanging of the bells" (Le Guin 1). This idyllic setting symbolizes utilitarianism's ideal outcome: the maximization of collective welfare. In addition, the beauty and charm of Omelas are not presented as superficial; instead, they represent a society who, by appearance, have achieved the ultimate goal of utilitarianism. This allows for the city to become realistic and encourages readers to compare the society they live in with the fabricated one of Omelas. The fact that the city is described to be perfect ultimately makes the moral paradox even more complex because both readers and citizens will grow attached to the beauty of Omelas, potentially influencing their level of selfishness when deciding on if they will leave or stay in the city. Furthermore, in the real world, this mirrors how nations and communities present themselves as thriving and successful through measures such as GDP while also concealing the exploitation and oppression of the individuals who enable that success. Le Guin uses this representation to convey that, similar to Omelas' beauty which persuades people to believe in its utopia, modern states use national pride and public celebrations to reinforce loyalty, even when their achievements rest on the hidden suffering of individuals. Defenders of utilitarianism could argue that if a society like Omelas can create success for its citizens, then some degree of hidden suffering is a reasonable sacrifice, especially if the majority of people are thriving. However, this logic overlooks the fact that the very existence of even one person's suffering calls questions on the ethical and moral foundation of the concept itself. The Universal Declaration of Human Rights states that "all human beings are born free and equal in dignity and rights" (United Nations article 1). Therefore, a utopia built on the denial of even a single person's basic rights is not a real utopia, but an illusion.

Le Guin's symbolism of the child, who is described as defective, referred to as an "it", and continuously mistreated by the people of Omelas, and its role in the city represent the individuals within utilitarian regimes who are marginalized and neglected. In fact, the city's well being, "the health of their children, the wisdom of their scholars, the skill of their makers, even the abundance of their harvest and the kindly weathers of their skies, depend wholly on this child's abominable misery" (Le Guin 3). The child functions as a direct symbol and embodiment of the utilitarian principle that the suffering of a child can be justified if it benefits the whole city. Specifically, the child's misery even determines the city's weather and harvest quality, which highlights the extent in which the community has grown to become dependent on the child. Additionally, the irony of the fact that the child's pain contributes to the "health of [Omelas'] children" underscores the cruelty of the world in which this utopia exists. Le Guin uses this symbol to compare the child to marginalized or oppressed groups whose suffering is normalized as the cost of societal success, such as low wage workers in dangerous industries or populations displaced by governments and industrial projects. Additionally, by reducing and degrading the child to an object of necessary suffering, Omelas illustrates how utilitarianism can reach moral and ethical extremes that cannot be justified. A utilitarian might claim that sacrificing one on the count of many is pragmatic and unavoidable. Peter Singer, a modern utilitarian philosopher, writes that "if it is in our power to prevent something bad from happening, without thereby sacrificing anything of comparable moral importance, we ought, morally, to do it" (Singer 1). However, history has shown how easily this logic can enable systematic abuse. In fact, the International Labor Organization found that despite Bangladesh's economic growth in recent years, there has not been much improvement in overall employment conditions. Specifically, "recent industrial accidents have brought global attention to severe occupational health and safety risks and also brought to the fore the issue of overall working conditions, including extremely low wages and weak social protection" ("Bangladesh Seeking Better Employment Conditions for Better Socioeconomic Outcomes" 6). Just as in Omelas, the suffering of the workers in Bangladesh is not a side effect, but a deliberate design to benefit the majority and trap the vulnerable and underprivileged in perpetual poverty and harm. Stripping away the rights of an individual is inherently dehumanizing and cannot be justified, even in pursuit of the greater good; Le Guin uses the child as a symbol to convey the significance of this idea and challenges the concept of utilitarianism. This encourages the reader to question whether any moral philosophy can stay ethical when its success is measured in lives suffering, which is a question further amplified by the complicity of the townspeople themselves.

The final symbol that Le Guin uses in "The Ones Who Walk Away from Omelas" is the people of Omelas who learn of the child's misery and choose to turn a blind eye, convincing themselves that its suffering is a necessary sacrifice for the city to thrive. Specifically, "they all know [the child] is there, all the people of Omelas. Some of them have come to see it, others are content merely to know it is there. They all know that it has to be there" (Le Guin 3). These townspeople symbolize the complicit bystanders of utilitarian systems: individuals who acknowledge injustice but justify it as an unavoidable cost. Additionally, the fact that these townspeople have been described to have children and are consistently humanized throughout the story further implies the cruelty and ignorance of bystanders in utilitarian systems. In the real world, this reflects how societies often tolerate systematic harm that certain people face, such as unpaid labor or unequal access to healthcare, as dismantling these systems would require sacrificing benefits enjoyed by the majority. Le Guin captures this selfishness and moral passiveness that can form when societies adopt a utilitarian ideology. Moreover, the suffering is acknowledged as regrettable, but remains unchallenged since it ensures the majority's happiness even though it neglects minorities. One could argue that the townspeople's complicity is a form of "moral disengagement". According to "Ethics Unwrapped" at the University of Texas in Austin, moral disengagement occurs when "we convince ourselves, without changing our moral standards, that our actions which violate those standards are permissible" (Bandura et al). Given that leaving Omelas would not free the child, the townspeople could have convinced themselves that they did not have the power to make a change, so they continue to participate in the vicious cycle of suffering for the child. On the other hand, in "States of Denial", sociologist Stanley Cohen argues that wilful ignorance is a choice and "knowing and not acting upon knowledge is the most extreme form of complicity" (Cohen 56). By refusing to confront or dismantle the system in Omelas, the citizens preserve it, making them not powerless, but actively responsible.

Le Guin's Omelas encourages readers to confront the cost of overall happiness and to recognize how easily comfort can depend on unseen or hidden cruelty. Through framing this ethical trade-off within a utopia, Le Guin removes any justification of utilitarianism and exposes the true meaning of the ideology. The unsettling and ultimate truth is that Omelas is not just a city in fantasy, but a mirror of real world systems where injustice lurks behind success. If we complicitly accept injustice as it is, then we become not only bystanders, but also creators of the harm. If our happiness is built upon the misery of others, then it is not happiness worth keeping.
excerpt: In 'The Ones Who Walk Away from Omelas', Ursula Le Guin uses symbolism within her creation of a utopia to represent the fundamental concept of utilitarianism...
